titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to his mother, Clara Mary. Davidson, 16 February 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother. James writes about the fighting and conditions in the trenches and his hope for leave within the next month as he feels he is in need of rest. He also requests that she order replacement items of kit to be sent to him.This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
16/2/16 My dearest Mother â Have not had time to write for the past few days â I can never get through half the things that I feel should be done. Was glad to get K's letter & to hear that all goes on well at home. Sorry about poor old Mrs MacIlwaine I suppose it was her old heart trouble. We have been having awful weather here â rain & storm. The trenches are in a terrible state â one sinks into mud & almost sticks in it. My boots are in a bad way so hope you were able to have my special branch boots sent off & that they will arrive soon. Would you please order another pair of the ordinary heavy Army
pattern from Manfield same exactly as the last pair only with nails in the soles. These can be sent out also as soon as ready. W We have not been shelled quite so much during the last 3 days which is rather a relief. One of my gun teams had a great escape day before yest. A shell dropped plump on the direction post of the gun just 25 yds away but did not burst. On Sat last the Germans were particularly vicious. When I was leaving my advanced H Q with the battery that has just been returned they put a shell on either side of the road unpleasantly close however a miss is just as good as a mile & so far my Comp has had great luck. If things go well & there is not much
doing I hope to be able to arrange to get leave early in March about 5th or 6th but of course will let you know more definitely later. Have been keeping wonderfully well but feel badly in need of a rest. With fondest love to all Your loving son Jim
On Active Service Mrs S C Davidson Seacourt Bangor Co Down Ireland 16/2/16
21st Feb/16
Glenbank
